lines.text: Section five. DNA synthesis, gene synthesis, and codon optimisation.
If sequencing is the technology for reading DNA, synthesis is the technology for writing it.
DNA synthesis falls into two broad categories that you should keep separated in your head: oligonucleotide synthesis, and gene synthesis.
Oligonucleotide synthesis is the production of short, single-stranded DNA molecules, typically 20 to 60 nucleotides long but sometimes up to 150 or so.
These are the primers that drive PCR, the probes that hybridise to specific targets, the linker sequences used in cloning.
They are also the building blocks for gene synthesis itself.
Oligonucleotide synthesis is a chemical process: solid-phase synthesis using phosphoramidite chemistry, in which the DNA chain is built one nucleotide at a time on a solid support, cycle by cycle.
It is done industrially, at automated 24-hour facilities, and like sequencing, the cost has been falling steadily for decades.
Gene synthesis is the more dramatic technology.
Gene synthesis is the custom commercial production of long, double-stranded DNA — from 200 base pairs up to many thousands.

You design a sequence, you place an order with a synthesis company, you wait a week or two, and you receive a tube of DNA that contains exactly the sequence you specified, sequence-verified to a high standard.
This is genuinely transformative for synthetic biology.

Twenty years ago, if you wanted a particular gene, you had to clone it from a natural source — find an organism that has it, isolate genomic DNA, PCR-amplify the gene, deal with whatever mutations and side products you got along the way.
Today, if you can write the sequence down, you can have it synthesised.
The price of gene synthesis, per base pair, has dropped on an even steeper exponential than sequencing.
How does gene synthesis work mechanically?

The desired gene is broken into many overlapping oligonucleotides — typically 40 to 60 nucleotides each — that together cover both strands of the final double-stranded product, with substantial overlap between adjacent oligonucleotides.
Those oligonucleotides are synthesised individually by phosphoramidite chemistry, then pooled together in a reaction where the overlaps allow them to anneal.
Ligase enzymes seal the gaps, or alternatively PCR amplification methods like progressive overlap extension PCR or thermodynamically balanced inside-out PCR fill in and amplify the assembly.
The result is the full-length double-stranded gene.
Because the assembly is imperfect, the product is then sequence-verified, and if there are errors, DNA repair enzymes are used to correct them.
Now to the most synthetic-biology-relevant benefit of gene synthesis: codon optimisation.

We mentioned codon usage bias in chapter one — the observation that different organisms prefer different codons for the same amino acid, and that a gene from one organism may translate poorly in another because the tRNAs for its preferred codons are scarce in the new host.
Before gene synthesis, codon optimisation was painful — you would have to PCR-mutate individual codons one at a time, which scales badly.

With gene synthesis, codon optimisation is trivial: you write down the protein sequence, you run a codon-optimisation algorithm that produces a DNA sequence using the new host's preferred codons, and you order that sequence.
A gene from a tobacco plant, when synthesised with E. coli codon optimisation, expresses dramatically better in E. coli than the native plant sequence would.

Gene synthesis also allows you to recode for other purposes: removing internal restriction sites that would interfere with cloning, removing internal secondary-structure-forming sequences, designing entirely artificial sequences that have never existed in nature.
Every one of these is a synthesis convenience that, twenty years ago, would have taken months of laboratory work.
Today, it is a checkbox on a synthesis-company order form.
If you think about what synthetic biology is doing — designing new biological systems from defined parts — gene synthesis is the technology that closes the loop between the dry lab and the wet lab.
Without it, every part of every design would have to be cloned by hand, and the field would still be a craft.
